setup_simulation_howto.user: How do I set up a simulation on the platform?
setup_simulation_howto.expected_output: To start a simulation workflow go to Workflows > Category: Simulation. Then choose a scale. After this, you will be prompted to select a model for simulation (depending on the scale you chose). You can then configure your simulation. First in Initialize, give it a Name and Description. You can then add different types of Stimuli and Recordings on the left hand side. You can change parameters of the stimuli or the target of the stimulus by creating a new Neuron Set and selcting it as the Neuron Set target of the stimulus. You can additionally make  modifications to the model (neuronal and synaptic modifications). Importantly, any variable of say a Stimulus, Neuron Set, Recording can be scanned over by pressing the plus icon in the field. Once your simulation is configured you can press "Generate simulations". This will generate the simulations in your campaign. You can then select simulations to launch.
setup_simulation_howto.ai_response:
I'll look up the platform documentation for simulation setup.## Quick overview — what you’ll do
1. Open **Workflows** → choose **Simulate**.  
2. Pick a **Type** (scale: Single neuron, Synaptome, Paired neurons, Small microcircuit, etc.).  
3. Select a **model** from the model picker (Public / Project tabs).  
4. Fill the **Configuration** (Info, Initialization, Stimuli, Recordings, Neuron/Synaptic manipulations, Events).  
5. Click **Generate simulation(s)** (validates & creates a campaign + one simulation per scanned parameter combination).  
6. Use the **Simulations** tab to launch, monitor, and download outputs.

Below is a step‑by‑step guide with the important details and best practices.

## 1) Start from the Workflows tab
- Open **Workflows** in the project top navigation.  
- Click the **Simulate** category card. This reveals the row of simulation Types (e.g., **Single neuron**, **Synaptome**, **Paired neurons (beta)**, **Small microcircuit (beta)**).  
- Click the Type card you want to start — this opens the model picker for that simulation Type.

## 2) Select a model
- In the model picker:
  - Toggle **Public / Project** to find models shared across the platform or in your project.
  - Use search/filters (brain region, name, metadata) to narrow results.
  - Click a row to open the preview panel (morphology, traces, metadata: Brain Region, species, counts).
  - Click **Use model** to accept it and open the configuration workspace (or **View details** first if you want more info).
- Note: nothing is submitted for compute until you click Generate — the picker and configuration are a draft session.

## 3) Configuration workspace — layout & flow
- Two top tabs: **Configuration** (fill campaign) and **Simulations** (populated after generation).
- Three-column layout:
  - Left: section navigator (Setup, Stimuli & Recordings, Manipulations, Events). Each section shows a status icon.
  - Middle: the section form you edit.
  - Right: live preview of the selected model (3D / straight views).
- Work top-to-bottom until all required sections show a green check — that enables the Generate button.

## 4) Required sections (what to fill)
### Setup
- **Info** (required)
  - **Campaign name** — human-readable unique name (required).
  - **Campaign description** — what you’re testing and why (required).
- **Initialization**
  - Simulation length (ms), **v_init** (mV), random seed, extracellular calcium (mM), etc.
  - Defaults are prefilled; change if needed.

### Stimuli & Recordings
- **Stimuli**
  - Click **Add stimulus** and choose type.
  - Two families of stimuli in beta flow:
    - *Relative* — amplitude expressed as % of each cell’s threshold (recommended for heterogeneous targets).
    - *Absolute* — amplitude in nA or conductance.
  - Common fields: Duration (ms), Timestamps (event or simulation start), Timestamp offset, Neuron set (if circuit-scale), Percentage of threshold / Amplitude.
  - You can add multiple stimuli (Stimulus 0, Stimulus 1, …).
- **Recordings**
  - Add recordings (e.g., somatic voltage full experiment or time window). Set timestep, start/end times as applicable.
  - Multiple independent recordings allowed.

### Circuit components (circuit-scale only)
- Define **Neuron sets** (e.g., presynaptic only) so stimuli/recordings/manipulations can target subpopulations.

### Manipulations
- Modify synapses or ion-channel parameters:
  - *Synaptic manipulations* (circuit flows) — scale conductances, disable pathways, change STP.
  - *Neuronal manipulations* (single-neuron flows) — change channel variables by SectionList or across full neuron.
- Optional by default.

### Events (Timestamps)
- Create named time markers:
  - **Single Timestamp** — marker at a specified ms.
  - **Regular Timestamps** — start, interval, count / end time.
- Use named events so multiple stimuli can reference the same timestamp.

## 5) Parameter scans (sweeps)
- Numeric fields have a small plus-circle icon. Click it to add extra values (range/array).
- Each extra value multiplies number of generated simulations — the campaign becomes the Cartesian product of scanned parameters.
- While any scan value is empty the owning section is yellow and generation is blocked.
- Tip: start with small sweeps (2 points) to validate before expanding.

## 6) Generate simulations
- When every required section shows a green check, the **Generate simulation(s)** button enables.
- Click it to validate the configuration against the simulation-type schema.
- On success:
  - A **campaign** is created.
  - One **Simulation** is generated per scanned-parameter combination.
  - The workspace switches to the **Simulations** tab.

## 7) Simulations tab — run and results
- Each simulation row lets you:
  - Launch (consume credits / run compute)
  - Monitor status (running, done, failed)
  - Download outputs (SONATA / logs / traces) when available
- Use the activity listing (Workflows page) later to re-open campaigns or view results.

## Tips & best practices
- **Preview the model first.** Always open the preview panel before Use model.
- **Use Relative stimuli** by default for heterogeneous neuron sets — they scale per-cell threshold automatically.
- **Name neuron sets and timestamps** before referencing them in stimuli/recordings.
- **Watch the left-column icons.** Yellow warnings are the authoritative blockers — fix those to enable generation.
- **Scan sparingly** at first — every scan multiplies simulations and compute cost.
- **Nothing is submitted** until you click Generate — you can safely close the draft session to discard changes.

## Checklist before generation
- [ ] **Campaign name** and **description** filled
- [ ] **Initialization** values set (or confirm defaults)
- [ ] Stimuli defined and targeted to correct neuron sets/events
- [ ] Recordings specified with appropriate sampling dt and windows
- [ ] Any manipulations defined (optional)
- [ ] Timestamps/events defined if used
- [ ] Parameter scans completed and all scan values filled

---

If you want, I can:
- Walk through creating a concrete example campaign step‑by‑step (tell me the Type and model you want to use).  
- Open the **Workflows** documentation pages I found for quick reference.

## How submission works (UI flow)
### 1) Start a workflow
- Open the Workflows tab in your project and choose Category → Type (e.g., Simulate → Paired neurons (beta) or Single neuron).

### 2) Build the campaign in the configuration form
- The configuration workspace is a guided, sectioned form (three-column layout):

simulate_simple_neuron.expected_output: Go to Workflows > Simulate > Single Neuron, select an ME-model, set the `max_time` parameter to 1000 ms (1 second) in the Experimental Setup, configure a stimulation protocol and recording location, then click Run. simulate_simple_neuron.ai_response:
I'll check the platform documentation for single-neuron simulation.## Short answer
Yes — you can run a single biophysical neuron simulation for 1 second on this platform. The Single Neuron simulation workflow supports exactly that (the default **Duration** is **1000 ms**).

---

## Step‑by‑step (web UI)
1. Open **Workflows** (top navigation).
2. Click the **Simulate** category card.
3. In the Type row choose **Single neuron** (or **Single neuron (beta)** if you prefer the beta UI).
4. In the model picker:
   - Select a suitable single‑neuron model (Public or Project tab).
   - Click **Use model** to enter the configuration workspace.
5. In the **Configuration** tab → **Setup → Initialization**:
   - Set **Duration** to **1000 ms** (default is 1000 ms already).
   - Optionally set timestep, temperature, initial voltage, random seed.
6. In **Stimuli & Recordings → Stimuli**:
   - Click **Add stimulus** and choose a current clamp (relative or absolute).
   - Set Duration, Timestamps (e.g., Simulation Start), and amplitude (e.g., % of threshold or nA).
7. In **Stimuli & Recordings → Recordings**:
   - Add a soma voltage recording (Full Experiment or Time Window).
   - Set sampling **Timestep** (for example 0.1 ms).
8. (Optional) Add manipulations, events (timestamps), or parameter scans.
9. Fill **Info** (Campaign name and description — required).
10. When every required section shows a green check, click **Generate simulation(s)**.
11. Switch to the **Simulations** tab, select the created simulation row and **Launch**/run it.
12. After the job finishes, view or download results from the Simulations tab.

---

## Notes & tips
- The **beta** flow provides richer stimulus/recording types (relative clamps, OU noise, scans, etc.) and an explicit parameter‑scan UI (plus‑circle on numeric fields).
- Many numeric fields (including **Duration**) can be scanned to generate multiple simulations (Cartesian product of scanned values). Be careful: scans multiply the number of runs.
- The **Initialization** section defaults include **Duration = 1000 ms**, so a 1‑second run typically requires no change there.
- Use *Relative* stimuli (percentage of threshold) to apply the same effective drive to heterogeneous neurons without manual tuning.
- Set an appropriate recording timestep (e.g., 0.1 ms) to capture the waveform resolution you need; smaller dt → larger output files.

---

## What you get after running
- Per‑simulation output files (voltage traces, configured recordings) available for download.
- Campaign and simulation metadata persisted as platform entities for provenance.

---

If you want, I can:
- Walk through the exact UI clicks for **Single neuron (beta)** vs the classic flow.
- Help pick a specific single‑neuron model from your project (I can open the model picker and show available me‑models). Which do you prefer?

I'll query the product documentation for \simulation".I'll summarize the platform documentation I found and give a clear step-by-step workflow for taking an ME-model (ME-model = morphology+electrical model) into simulation, plus how to locate and use the simulation notebooks that run/inspect ME-model simulations.

## Summary of where this is described
- Platform documentation: \The Workflows tab\ and \Designing a Simulation\ (product: circuit-simulation) describe the full simulate flow (pick Simulate → choose a simulation Type → pick a model → configure campaign → generate simulations → run and view results).
- The UI contains a top-level tab bar where **Workflows**, **Data**, **Notebooks**, and **Reports** are sibling views — so notebooks for running/inspecting simulations live under the **Notebooks** tab of your Virtual Lab / project.

Now the practical step‑by‑step.

## Step 1 — Open the Simulate workflow
1. In your Virtual Lab project, open the top navigation and click **Workflows**.
2. In the Category row click **Simulate**. This reveals the simulation Types row (Single neuron, Synaptome, beta flavors, paired neurons, etc.).

Notes:
- The Workflows view also lists existing workflows (independent filters) and sits next to **Notebooks** in the tab bar.

## Step 2 — Choose the correct simulation Type for your ME-model
1. Pick the Type that matches your goal:
   - For a single ME-model use **Single neuron (beta)** or **Single neuron** (depending on available options).
   - For circuit/multi-cell ME-models use the matching circuit-level Type (e.g., **Paired neurons (beta)** or **Small microcircuit (beta)**).
2. Clicking the Type starts a model picker scoped to that Type (no compute yet).

## Step 3 — Select your ME-model in the model picker
1. In the model picker switch Public / Project tabs or use Search/Filters to find your ME-model.
2. Click the row to open the preview panel (shows morphology and trace thumbnails, brain region, m-/e-type, validated flag).
3. When satisfied click **Use model** to open the simulation configuration workspace (this opens a session; still no compute).

Important: for ME-models the picker and preview explicitly show morphology and trace thumbnails and *Validated (True/False)* — use those to confirm you selected the ME-model you want.

## Step 4 — Configure the simulation campaign
The configuration view is a three-column layout:
- Left column: section navigator (Setup, Stimuli & Recordings, Manipulations, Events). Each section shows a status icon (green check when complete).
- Middle column: the active form for the selected section.
- Right column: live model preview (3D / straight views, thumbnails).

Minimum required steps:
1. Setup → Info: provide **Campaign name** and **Campaign description** (both required).
2. Setup → Initialization: set *Duration, timestep, initial voltage, random seed,* etc. (defaults provided).
3. Stimuli & Recordings: add stimuli (relative or absolute current clamps, OU processes, etc.) and recordings you need.
   - For ME-models use *Relative* clamps to scale to each cell's threshold if appropriate.
4. Manipulations: optional — modify ion-channel or synaptic parameters if needed.
5. Events/Timestamps: define named time markers that stimuli/recordings can reference.

Tips:
- The configuration shows yellow warnings for missing required fields; the **Generate simulation(s)** button remains disabled until all required sections are green.
- Use the plus-circle next to numeric fields to define parameter scans (each extra value multiplies the number of generated simulations).

## Step 5 — Generate simulations and run them
1. When all required sections are green, click **Generate simulation(s)** (creates a Campaign and one Simulation per parameter-combination).
2. The workspace switches to the **Simulations** tab for that campaign. Each Simulation row lets you:
   - Launch (run) the simulation
   - Monitor status (running / done / error)
   - Download outputs when finished

The documentation describes that running a simulation consumes credits and that the credits counter is visible in the UI.

## Where to find and use Notebooks for ME-model simulations
- The platform UI includes a **Notebooks** tab next to **Workflows** (top-level tab bar described in the docs). Use that tab to:
  - Open or create notebooks associated with your project / virtual lab
  - Re-open analysis notebooks that inspect simulation outputs
- Typical workflow:
  1. Run the simulation(s) from the campaign **Simulations** tab.
  2. Once results are produced, go to **Notebooks** (top-level tab).
  3. Open an existing simulation-analysis notebook (if your project provides one) or create a new notebook that loads the simulation outputs (SONATA or the platform's result artifacts).
  4. Notebooks are the place to run custom analyses, visualize traces, compute metrics, or launch further post-processing.

Notes from docs:
- The Workflows page indicates the Notebooks tab is the sibling location for further analysis; the per-type guides describe that after generation the **Simulations** tab shows results which you can then inspect or download — notebooks are the standard place to run more advanced analyses on those outputs.

## Quick checklist you can follow now
- [ ] Workflows → Simulate → pick the Type matching ME-model
- [ ] Model picker → preview the ME-model → Use model
- [ ] Fill Setup (name + description) and Initialization
- [ ] Add Stimuli & Recordings and any Manipulations
- [ ] Add Events/Timestamps if needed; set parameter scans if sweeping
- [ ] Generate simulation(s) → Simulations tab → launch runs
- [ ] After runs finish: Notebooks tab → open/create notebooks to load and analyze outputs

If you want, I can:
- Walk you through configuring a specific ME-model step‑by‑step if you give me the ME-model name/ID (I can list the fields you’ll need to fill and suggest stimulus/recording settings).
- Show how to open a notebook template for analyzing single-neuron ME-model outputs (if your project has template notebooks available, I can locate them).

synaptic_input_meaning.user: What does the "synaptic input" on the current page mean? Is it a signal input from a specified position?
synaptic_input_meaning.expected_output: In the current Simulation -> Synaptime you can stimulate existing sets of synapses on the synaptome. You can build such a synaptome with specific locations, through Build > Synaptome.
synaptic_input_meaning.ai_response:
I'll search the platform documentation for \synaptic\ to find the definition and how it's used on the page.I'll summarize the documentation results and then answer your question directly.

## Short answer
On the platform, **\synaptic input\** means input that arrives via synapses (i.e., activity delivered through the model's synaptic connections). It is not primarily a geometric \signal injected at a spatial position\ like a point current injection — instead it is activity delivered through presynaptic→postsynaptic connections (either from neurons already present in the circuit/model or from externally defined spike-train stimuli). You target synaptic input by selecting neuron sets, populations, or connection sources (not by clicking a 3‑D location).

## Evidence from the docs (high level)
- Circuit simulations always include synaptic connections and synaptic mechanisms (e.g., ProbAMPANMDA_EMS, ProbGABAAB_EMS). Synapses are part of the circuit representation and carry inputs between neurons.  
  - Source: \Circuit simulations ... Synaptic connections: Edges between neurons with synaptic mechanisms\ and \Circuit simulations automatically include synaptic mechanisms."
- There is a dedicated Synaptome / Synaptic manipulations capability: you can modify synapse properties (scale conductances, block pathways, toggle plasticity) — i.e., synapses are modelled as connection objects you can change.  
  - Source: \Synaptic manipulations lets you modify synaptic properties ... For example, scaling conductances, blocking specific pathways, or toggling short-term plasticity."
- Stimuli that produce synaptic input include spike‑based stimuli (Poisson, synchronous, sinusoidal Poisson) or targeting specific neuron sets; stimuli can target neuron sets or presynaptic populations so the delivered input travels through synapses to downstream cells.  
  - Source: \Stimuli: ... Spike-based stimuli: Poisson, synchronous, sinusoidal Poisson. Targeted stimuli: Can target specific neuron sets."
- The Synaptome simulation type (and paired neuron / circuit simulations) models synaptic inputs in addition to current injection — i.e., synaptic input is a distinct input channel from somatic current clamps.  
  - Source: \Synaptome — single neuron with synaptic inputs in addition to current injection."

## Practical implications — what you can do on the page
- If you want to *drive a neuron via synapses*:
  - Use the circuit model or synaptome model that has presynaptic sources and synapses defined.
  - Add a spike-based stimulus (Poisson, synchronous, etc.) and target it at the presynaptic neuron set or population. Those spikes will be delivered through the model's synapses to the postsynaptic targets.
- If you want to *change how synaptic inputs behave*:
  - Use Synaptic manipulations to scale synaptic conductances, disable pathways, or toggle plasticity. Those changes apply to connection objects, not to a 3‑D coordinate.
- If you want a *spatially localized stimulation* (e.g., inject current at a particular compartment/position):
  - Use a current-clamp stimulus targeted to the soma or, for finer spatial control, use the single-cell ME-model tools (some stimulus types are somatic clamps). Circuit-level stimuli are usually addressed by neuron set or population rather than exact 3‑D coordinates.

## One-sentence clarification
\Synaptic input\ = signals arriving via synapses (from presynaptic neurons or external spike trains routed through synapses), targeted by neuron sets/populations or connection sources — not by specifying a position in 3‑D space.

Would you like a step-by-step walkthrough on the page to add a spike‑based synaptic stimulus or to set up a synaptic manipulation for a particular neuron set?

run_simulation_file.user: How do I run a simulation file like "passive_neuron_1dend.py" on the platform?
run_simulation_file.expected_output: You can run your own Jupyter notebooks on the platform. Go to Notebooks and click "Open Jupyter Hub". For simulations however, we encourage user to use our Simulation workflows (Workflows > Category: Simulation). These workflows are designed to maximise experimental flexibility and remove the complexities of designing and running a simulation.
run_simulation_file.ai_response:
I'll look up the platform documentation on running simulations. I'll search the product documentation for \simulation".I'll search the documentation for guidance about running custom simulation scripts or single-neuron workflows.I'll search the docs for terms like \upload", \custom script", or \python file\ to see if the platform supports running user script files directly.## Short answer — the supported way
You don't run an arbitrary Python file directly. On the Open Brain Platform you run simulations by creating a *simulation campaign* from a registered model via the Workflows → Simulate flow (e.g., **Single neuron** or **Single neuron (beta)**). To run something named like **passive_neuron_1dend.py** you must first make it available as a platform model artifact (or recreate the model using the platform's ME-model/ME‑model importer or OBI-one workflows). Then pick that model in the Simulate workflow, configure the stimuli/recordings, generate the simulation campaign and run it.

Below are the concrete options and step‑by‑step instructions.

---

## Options depending on what your file is

- **If `passive_neuron_1dend.py` is (or can be turned into) a registered ME-model / platform model entity**  
  - Upload/register the model into your Project (see “Uploading data to a project”).  
  - Open Workflows → Simulate → choose the appropriate Type (e.g., **Single neuron** or **Single neuron (beta)**).  
  - In the model picker, switch to the Project tab, find and **Use model**.  
  - Fill the Configuration form (Info, Initialization, Stimuli, Recordings, etc.).  
  - Click **Generate simulation(s)** and then run the created simulation(s) from the Simulations tab.

- **If `passive_neuron_1dend.py` is a standalone Python script (not a platform model)**  
  - The platform does not run arbitrary one-off Python scripts directly via the Simulate UI. You should either:
    - Convert/package the script into a platform-compatible model entity (an ME-model) and register it, then follow the steps above; or
    - Reimplement the experiment in the Simulate workflow using an equivalent platform model from the model picker, or build a new model via the Build workflows (if applicable).
  - If you intend to run programmatic pipelines, consider using the platform’s programmatic tools (OBI‑one) to create and run campaigns — the docs describe programmatic workflows (e.g., OBI-one tasks) for supported build workflows.

---

## Detailed step-by-step (UI path)

1. Go to your Project in the Virtual Lab.
2. Upload/register the model:
   - Open the Explore / Upload area and use the Upload wizard (select Project mode) to add your model artifact to the project. (See the docs: “Uploading data to a project”.)
   - Ensure the uploaded artifact is registered as a model entity the Simulate picker can list.
3. Open Workflows (top navigation) → click **Simulate**.
4. Select the simulation Type card that matches your model (e.g., **Single neuron (beta)**).
5. In the model picker:
   - Use the Project tab to find your uploaded model.
   - Click the row → preview → **Use model**.
6. Configure the campaign:
   - Fill **Info** (campaign name & description) — required.
   - Verify **Initialization** (duration, timestep, seed, etc.).
   - Add **Stimuli** (e.g., somatic current clamp) and **Recordings** (soma voltage).
   - (Optional) Add manipulations, neuron sets, or events (timestamps).
   - Use parameter scans via the plus-circle controls if you want multiple runs.
   - Make sure every required section shows the green check.
7. Click **Generate simulation(s)**:
   - The platform validates and creates a SimulationCampaign and one Simulation per scanned-parameter combination.
8. Open the **Simulations** tab:
   - Launch the simulation(s).
   - Monitor progress and download outputs when finished.

(See the docs “The Workflows tab”, “Designing a Simulation”, and the “Configuring a beta simulation” guide for screenshots and details.)

---

## Programmatic / developer path
- If you prefer programmatic control or need to run batch jobs, use the platform’s OBI-one programmatic workflows to construct campaigns and tasks. The documentation includes examples and task descriptions (for example, the ion-channel build workflow is implemented in OBI-one tasks). Use OBI-one when you need to automate upload → configure → generate → run pipelines.

---

## If you want help from me
Tell me which of these matches your situation and I’ll walk you through the exact next action:
- A — “passive_neuron_1dend.py is an ME-model file / ready to upload” — I’ll give the precise upload steps and the fields to set in the picker.
- B — “It’s a standalone Python script I can’t convert” — I’ll outline how to convert it (what the platform expects) or suggest the closest workflow.
- C — “I want a programmatic OBI-one example to create and run the campaign” — I’ll pull the OBI-one docs and show an example approach.

Which option would you like to proceed with?

I'll check the platform documentation on simulation capabilities and how to model anatomical alterations. I'll search the docs for \simulation".I'll search the docs for  rain\ and  rain region\ to confirm platform support for whole-brain or anatomical simulations.## Short answer

multi_neuron_simulation.ai_response:
I'll look up the platform documentation on running simulations.## Overview — how to run a multi‑neuron simulation on the Open Brain Platform

Below is a concise step‑by‑step guide (UI + recommended settings) for creating and running a multi‑neuron simulation (e.g., **Paired neurons (beta)** or **Small microcircuit (beta)**). I’ll first list the high‑level steps, then expand each with the important fields, tips, and what to watch for.

---

## 1) Start a simulation workflow
- Open the **Workflows** tab in your project.
- Click the **Simulate** category card.
- In the Type row choose a circuit‑scale type (for multi‑neuron): e.g., **Paired neurons (beta)** or **Small microcircuit (beta)**.
  - Clicking the Type card opens the model picker (no compute used yet).

---

## 2) Pick a model
- Use the **model picker** to choose a circuit/circuit‑scale model:
  - Switch Public / Project tabs to find models in the project or platform.
  - Use Search / Filters (brain region, #neurons, #synapses, validated) to narrow results.
- Click a row to open the preview panel (morphology + legend + metadata).
- Click **Use model** to accept the model and enter the configuration workspace.

---

## 3) Configure the simulation campaign (Configuration tab)
The configuration workspace uses a three‑column layout (left: section navigator; middle: forms; right: live preview of the model).

Required sections you must fill:
- **Info (Setup)** — *required*
  - **Campaign name** (human readable)
  - **Campaign description**
- **Initialization (Setup)** — set global simulation parameters (defaults prefilled)
  - Duration (ms), Extracellular Ca2+ (mM), initial V (mV), random seed, etc.

Experiment design sections:
- **Stimuli (Stimuli & Recordings)**  
  - Click **Add stimulus** and choose a stimulus type (Relative or Absolute).
  - For circuits you can target a **Neuron set** (e.g., presynaptic only).
  - Common fields: Duration, Timestamps (or Event), Timestamp offset, Amplitude (% of threshold for Relative, nA for Absolute).
  - Waveform‑specific params (OU τ, SD, frequency, pulse width) appear when relevant.
- **Recordings (Stimuli & Recordings)**  
  - Add soma voltage / compartment recordings; set timestep (dt) and time windows.
- **Circuit components / Neuron sets (Circuit types)**  
  - Define custom neuron sets (e.g., presynaptic, postsynaptic, layer subsets) so stimuli/recordings can target them.
- **Manipulations (Synaptic manipulations for circuits)**  
  - Optional: scale synaptic weights, disconnect pathways, modify short‑term plasticity, change Mg2+.
- **Events / Timestamps**  
  - Define named time markers (Single or Regular timestamps) and reference them from Stimuli/Recordings.

Important UI behavior:
- Each section shows a status icon: **green check** = valid, **yellow warning** = required fields missing.
- The **Generate simulation(s)** button at bottom left is enabled only when all required sections are green.

---

## 4) Parameter scans (sweeps)
- Numeric inputs have a small plus-circle that turns the field into a scan (add multiple values).
- The campaign generates one simulation per combination (Cartesian product) of scanned parameter values.
- Tip: scan sparingly — scans multiply the number of simulations rapidly.

---

## 5) Generate the campaign and simulations
- Click **Generate simulation(s)** when the form is valid.
- Platform validates the campaign schema, then creates:
  - A **SimulationCampaign** (campaign metadata)
  - One or more **Simulations** (one per scanned combination)
- The workspace switches to the **Simulations** tab.

---

## 6) Launch runs, monitor, and get results (Simulations tab)
- In the Simulations tab:
  - Select simulation rows to **Launch** (start execution), monitor status (queued / running / done), and cancel if needed.
  - When finished you can **View**, **Download** outputs (SONATA traces, voltage recordings, spikes, metadata).
- Check estimated credit usage before launching (Credits counter / estimates are visible).

---

## Best practices and tips
- Use **Relative stimuli** by default for heterogeneous populations — they scale to each cell’s threshold.
- Define **Neuron sets** before referencing them in stimuli/recordings.
- Use **Events/Timestamps** to coordinate simultaneous stimuli across neuron sets.
- Validate smaller test runs first (e.g., reduce duration or use a two‑point scan) before sweeping many params.
- Keep an eye on the **section status icons** — those determine whether generation is allowed.
- Monitor your **Virtual Lab/project credits**; complex circuits / long durations consume more compute.

---

## Quick checklist (minimal for a runnable multi‑neuron campaign)
- [x] Selected circuit model (Use model)
- [x] Info: campaign name + description (required)
- [x] Initialization: duration, v_init, random seed (defaults OK)
- [x] Stimuli: at least one stimulus (target correct neuron set)
- [x] Recordings: at least one recording (e.g., soma voltage)
- [x] (Optional) Scans: added only if you intend multiple simulations
- [x] All required sections show green → click **Generate simulation(s)**
- [x] Simulations tab → Launch → monitor → download results

---

If you want, I can:
- Walk you through configuring a specific multi‑neuron model step‑by‑step (tell me which type: Paired neurons or Small microcircuit).

## Options and trade-offs

### 1) Small microcircuit (recommended)
- Scope: tens to a few hundred neurons (microcircuit motifs), supports circuit-level connectivity and synaptic manipulations.
- Why: captures network effects (balance of excitation / inhibition, population firing, propagation of hyperexcitability after TBI, or reduced connectivity in aging).

### 2) Paired neurons
- Scope: two connected neurons (pre/post), useful for mechanistic dissection of synapse-level changes (e.g., STP, synaptic failure probability).

- Platform support: **Paired neurons (beta)** is available.

### 3) Single neuron / Synaptome
- Scope: detailed single-cell electrophysiology with synaptic inputs.
- Why: isolate cellular excitability changes (ion-channel expression, input-output changes with aging/TBI).

## How to represent “Aging” and “TBI” in simulations (practical manipulations you can apply on the platform)

Suggested implementation plan (you can pick elements):

- Create a baseline hippocampal microcircuit model (e.g., CA1 microcircuit).
- Aging manipulations (examples to implement as parameter changes / scans):
  - Reduce excitatory synapse counts or probability of connection (simulate synapse loss).
  - Decrease excitatory synaptic conductance (scale synaptic g values).
  - Adjust ion-channel densities (e.g., reduce Na+ or K+ channel conductance to change excitability) via neuronal manipulations.
  - Optionally: increase membrane time constants / leak conductance if you want slower dynamics.
- TBI manipulations (examples):
  - Locally increase excitatory synaptic strengths or decrease inhibition to simulate post-injury hyperexcitability.
  - Increase synapse failure / stochastic release variability or change short-term plasticity parameters.
  - Implement partial axonal/synaptic lesions by removing a fraction of connections or neuron subsets.
  - Change extracellular parameters (where available in model) or add focal current injections to mimic injury-driven depolarization.
- Define the same stimuli and recording protocol for all conditions so outputs are comparable (e.g., spontaneous activity baseline, evoked input, voltage and synaptic current recordings).
- Use parameter scans to explore severity (e.g., 10%, 30%, 50% synapse loss) and time since injury (if modeled).

  - **Single neurons** (biophysical / ME-model style).

## Short answer
Yes — you *can* differentiate different neuron types in human substantia nigra and cingulate cortex from synaptome-style data, but whether you *can do it for a given dataset* depends on what metadata and measurements the dataset contains (molecular markers, cell-type labels, synapse-type annotations, spatial resolution). If you want, I can search the platform database for human synaptome / synapse‑level datasets that cover the substantia nigra and cingulate cortex and report what cell-type labels and synapse annotations they provide. Shall I look up available datasets now?

---

## What \differentiating neuron types from synaptome data\ means
- Synaptome data = measurements and annotations at the level of synapses (synapse locations, synapse molecular composition, counts/densities, sometimes ultrastructure).  
- To infer or directly label neuron types from synaptome data you typically need one or more of:
  - **Cell-type (molecular) markers** co-measured with synapses (e.g., immunolabels for dopamine transporter, GAD67, vGluT, cell‑type protein markers).
  - **Anatomical location and laminar position** (e.g., cingulate cortex layers give strong priors about excitatory vs inhibitory and projection classes).
  - **Connectivity patterns** (pre‑ vs post‑synaptic targets, projection targets, or partner synapse identity).
  - **Morphological reconstructions** linked to synapse locations (axon/dendrite shape, spine morphology).
  - **Electrophysiology / e‑type or transcriptomic labels** matched to synaptome (multi-modal datasets where cells are profiled by transcriptomics/physiology and synapses are mapped).
- If a synaptome dataset contains explicit cell IDs or neuron labels (m-types, e-types, molecular tags), differentiation is direct. If it only contains synapse coordinates and molecular staining but no cell IDs, you can infer types probabilistically (e.g., label synapses as dopaminergic in substantia nigra if they co‑localize with TH/DAT signal).

---

## Region-specific notes (human)

### Substantia nigra (human)
- Key cell types relevant to identification:
  - **Dopaminergic neurons** (major cell class; TH/DAT immunolabeling is highly informative).
  - GABAergic projection/interneurons.
  - Glia and modulatory afferents (serotonergic, cholinergic inputs).
- Practical indicators in synaptome data:
  - Presence of **tyrosine hydroxylase (TH)** / **DAT** signal at soma/axon terminals → identifies dopaminergic neurons/terminals.
  - Synapse ultrastructure + location (e.g., synapse types onto dopaminergic somata vs dendrites) can help separate input classes.
  - If dataset includes long-range projection IDs, you can distinguish SN pars compacta vs pars reticulata circuits.

### Cingulate cortex (human)
- Key cell classes:
  - **Excitatory pyramidal neurons** (layer- and projection-specific; e.g., layer II/III vs V/VI).
  - **Inhibitory interneurons** (PV, SST, VIP, etc. — often distinguished by molecular markers).
  - Layer assignment and laminar synapse distribution are strong cues.
- Practical indicators:
  - Co-staining for interneuron markers (PV, SST, VIP) in synapse vicinities or cell bodies → direct interneuron subtype identification.
  - Laminar position (e.g., counts of synapses in **layer** coordinates) helps resolve excitatory projection classes.
  - Spine vs shaft synapses, and receptor composition (AMPA, NMDA subunits, GABA receptors) can indicate excitatory vs inhibitory origin.

---

## What the platform needs to provide for reliable differentiation
For the Open Brain Platform (or any synaptome resource) to let you distinguish neuron types in these human regions, the dataset should include at least one of the following:
- Per-synapse molecular labels (e.g., immunofluorescence channels for TH, vGluT, GAD, PV, SST, VIP).
- Mapping of synapses to identified neuronal processes/cell IDs (morphology with synapse attachments).
- Cell-type annotations (m-type/e-type/marker labels or transcriptomic matching).
- Precise spatial registration to an atlas (so you can locate synapses to cingulate layers or SN subregions).
- Metadata describing specimen species (human), region, and sample preparation.

If a dataset lacks these, differentiation may still be possible but will be probabilistic and limited.

---

## Practical recommendations / analysis plan
If you want me to check the platform for available synaptome or synapse-resolved datasets in human substantia nigra and cingulate cortex, I can:
1. Search the database for synaptome / synapse annotation datasets restricted to *Homo sapiens* and the two regions.  
2. For each dataset found report:
   - **Dataset name / ID**, availability (public/private)
   - Which molecular markers are present (TH, PV, SST, vGluT, GAD, etc.)
   - Whether synapses are mapped to cell morphologies or cell IDs
   - Spatial resolution and atlas registration (layer/subregion coverage)
   - Total_items count and links to the entities so you can open them
3. If none are available, I can search for related circuit or morphology datasets (e.g., single-cell morphologies with synapse annotations) and/or recommend experiments/markers to collect.

brain_structures_movement.user: What are the structures of the brain mainly involved in movement?
brain_structures_movement.ai_response:
## Overview — main brain structures for movement

Movement is produced and coordinated by a distributed network spanning cortex, subcortex, brainstem and spinal cord. Below is a concise map of the principal structures and their core roles.

### Cerebral cortex
- **Primary motor cortex (M1; precentral gyrus)**  
  - Direct execution of voluntary movements; somatotopic (motor) map; issues descending commands via corticospinal tract.
- **Premotor cortex**  
  - Planning and selection of movements in response to external cues; preparation of motor programs.
- **Supplementary motor area (SMA)**  
  - Internal planning, sequencing, bimanual coordination, and initiation of self-generated movements.
- **Primary somatosensory cortex (S1)**  
  - Provides tactile and proprioceptive feedback used to adjust motor output.

### Basal ganglia (extrapyramidal system)
- **Caudate nucleus & Putamen (together: striatum)**  
  - Input stage; integrate cortical signals and select motor programs.
- **Globus pallidus (internal/external segments)**  
  - Major output (GPi) influences thalamus; regulates movement initiation and inhibition.
- **Substantia nigra (pars compacta and pars reticulata)**  
  - Dopaminergic modulation (SNc) and output gating (SNr); critical for movement vigor and selection.
- **Subthalamic nucleus (STN)**  
  - Modulatory node important for stopping/inhibiting actions.

Function: action selection, scaling, suppression of unwanted movements; dysfunction → parkinsonism, chorea, dystonia.

### Cerebellum
- **Cerebellar cortex and deep nuclei (dentate, interposed, fastigial)**  
  - Error correction, timing, coordination, motor learning; integrates sensory feedback to fine-tune ongoing movements.
Function: precision, adaptation, balance and posture.

### Thalamus
- **Motor-related thalamic nuclei (e.g., ventrolateral nucleus)**  
  - Relay motor information between basal ganglia/cerebellum and cortex; part of cortico‑basal ganglia and cortico‑cerebellar loops.

### Brainstem and midbrain
- **Red nucleus**  
  - Involved in motor coordination (rubrospinal influence especially in non-primate mammals).
- **Reticular formation**  
  - Posture, locomotor patterns, modulation of tone, integration of multisensory motor signals.
- **Vestibular nuclei**  
  - Balance and head‑position reflexes; influence posture and eye movements.
- **Cranial nerve motor nuclei**  
  - Control of face, eye, tongue, swallowing and speech muscles.

### Descending pathways and spinal cord
- **Corticospinal (pyramidal) tract**  
  - Major voluntary motor pathway from cortex to spinal motor neurons; crucial for fine dexterous control (especially in primates).
- **Extrapyramidal tracts (vestibulospinal, reticulospinal, rubrospinal)**  
  - Posture, gait, automatic patterns and gross limb movements.
- **Spinal cord motor circuits (central pattern generators, motor neurons, interneurons)**  
  - Execute rhythmic movements (walking), reflexes, and final muscle activation.

### Sensory & proprioceptive systems (supporting roles)
- **Muscle spindles, Golgi tendon organs** → dorsal root ganglia → spinal cord → cerebellum and cortex  
  - Provide feedback required to adjust and refine movement.
- **Visual and vestibular systems**  
  - Guide orientation, balance and visually‑directed movements.

## Functional organization (brief)
- **Planning & initiation:** premotor areas, SMA, basal ganglia  
- **Execution & direct control:** M1 → corticospinal tract → spinal motor neurons  
- **Coordination & error correction:** cerebellum, brainstem nuclei  
- **Modulation & gating:** basal ganglia, thalamus  
- **Reflexes & pattern generation:** spinal cord, brainstem

If you want, I can:
- Provide a diagram linking these nodes,
- Summarize clinical syndromes (e.g., Parkinson’s, cerebellar ataxia) and which structures are affected,
- Or give a more detailed pathway map (e.g., corticospinal route, basal ganglia circuits). Which would you like next?

entitysdk_library.ai_response:
I'll look up the official documentation for the OBI entitysdk Python package.## Summary — What is the **entitysdk** library?

**entitysdk** is the official Python client library for interacting with the Open Brain Platform’s *entitycore* service. It provides a type-safe, Pydantic-based interface for searching, creating, updating, and managing neuroscience-related entities (e.g., cell morphologies, circuits, simulations) and their associated file assets via the platform’s REST API.

---

## Main purpose and design goals

- Provide a simple, programmatic interface to the entitycore REST API.
- Offer *type-safe models* (Pydantic) for entity payloads and responses.
- Handle authentication and *project context* management.
- Facilitate file uploads/downloads (including multipart/streaming for large files).
- Support common workflows for neuroscience data (search, CRUD, asset management).

---

## Key features

- **Client class** that centralizes API URL, project context, and token management.
- **Pydantic models** for all major entity types (e.g., CellMorphology, Circuit, Simulation).
- File handling helpers:
  - Single file uploads
  - Directory uploads
  - Multipart uploads for large assets
  - Download helpers for nested data structures
- Support for multiple deployment environments (staging, production).
- Example notebooks and templates for common analysis workflows (e.g., analysis notebook initialization).

---

## Common use cases

- Programmatically search the platform for entities (morphologies, recordings, circuits).
- Upload model files, morphologies, or simulation outputs as assets attached to entities.
- Create or update project-scoped entities (with proper project context).
- Initialize analysis notebooks programmatically (set runtime info, dependencies).
- Integrate the Open Brain Platform from Python-based analysis pipelines or notebooks.

---

## Installation

From the repository README:

```bash
pip install entitysdk
pip install obi-auth
```

(obi-auth helps with token management for authenticating the client.)

---

## Basic import & client initialization example

The library centers on the `Client` class. Example (from the analysis notebook example):

```python
import os
from entitysdk.client import Client
from entitysdk.common import ProjectContext

# Example project context (UUIDs)
project_context = ProjectContext(
    virtual_lab_id=\a98b7abc-fc46-4700-9e3d-37137812c730",
    project_id=\0dbced5f-cc3d-488a-8c7f-cfb8ea039dc6",
)

# Token (e.g., from env var or obi-auth)
token = os.getenv(\ACCESS_TOKEN", \XXX")

# API URL (local example shown)
entitycore_api_url = \http://127.0.0.1:8000"

# Initialize client
client = Client(api_url=entitycore_api_url, project_context=project_context, token_manager=token)
```

After initialization you can call client methods to search, fetch, create, upload assets, etc., using the Pydantic models available in `entitysdk.models`.

---

## Quick-start workflow (typical steps)

1. Install and import entitysdk.
2. Initialize `Client` with API URL, `ProjectContext`, and auth token.
3. Use typed models from `entitysdk.models` to build requests.
4. Call client methods to search, create, update entities or upload assets.
5. Use file helpers for large or nested asset uploads/downloads.

---

## Example resources & docs

- Official package repo (examples, notebooks, README):  
  [Analysis notebook example in repository](https://github.com/openbraininstitute/entitysdk/blob/main/examples/06_analysis_notebook.ipynb)  
  (Shows initialization, runtime specification types, and client usage.)

- README / Quick Start (installation and usage):  
  https://github.com/openbraininstitute/entitysdk/blob/main/README.md

- Context7-hosted package docs summary used by the platform:  
  https://context7.com/openbraininstitute/entitysdk/llms.txt

---

If you’d like, I can:
- Show a short example of searching for a specific entity type (e.g., CellMorphology) using the SDK,
- Open the example notebook and extract the initialization + upload example into a ready-to-run cell,
- Or point to specific model classes (Pydantic types) you plan to use. Which would you prefer?

### Notebook: Simulate one ME-model neuron (Single neuron (beta) — starter)
Purpose: run 1 s simulation, record somatic voltage; apply a current ramp that can reveal hyperexcitability (useful starting test for seizure-relevant excitability).

   - **Initial Voltage (mV):** **-80**  
   - **Random Seed:** **1**

   Stimuli & Recordings → Stimuli
   - Click **Add stimulus** → choose **Linear Somatic Current Clamp (Relative)** (a relative ramp)
   - Parameters:
     - **Duration (ms):** 600  
     - **Timestamps:** Simulation Start (0 ms)  
     - **Timestamp offset (ms):** 200  — so the ramp runs from 200 ms → 800 ms  
     - **Percentage of Threshold Current (start):** **0** (%)  
     - **Percentage of Threshold Current (end):** **150** (%)  — ramp from 0% → 150% of threshold
     - Neuron set: (default: All Biophysical Neurons) — OK for single neuron

## 6) Quick tips & safety for seizure-relevant experiments
- Use *Relative* current clamps (percent of threshold) when possible — this avoids manual amplitude tuning.

I'll look up the platform documentation to give an authoritative summary of purpose, target users, and real-world applications. I'll query the documentation.## Summary — Purpose, Audience, and Real‑world Applications

### Purpose of the Open Brain Platform
- **Primary goal:** Provide an integrated, provenance-aware platform to explore, build, and run biophysically-detailed brain models and associated data.  
- **Key capabilities (from documentation):**
  - Discover and browse experimental data, computational models, simulations, and literature (**Explore Data Guide**).
  - Provide standardized workflows (OBI‑one) for building and running modeling pipelines: ion channel model building, neuron skeletonization, paired-neuron simulations, circuit simulations, etc. (**OBI-one / workflow overviews**).
  - Store, index and manage provenance for models and results; support parameter scans and reproducible workflows.
  - Offer programmatic APIs and web interfaces for designing and executing simulation campaigns (single-cell, paired, circuit).

### Who it is designed for
- **Computational neuroscientists** who develop, run, and analyze biophysically-detailed neuron and circuit models.
- **Experimental neuroscientists** who want to explore, reuse or compare their experimental data (morphologies, electrophysiology, synapse data) with models and simulations.
- **Model builders & method developers** (ion channel modellers, morphology/skeletonization experts) who need standardized pipelines and provenance for publishing and reusing modeling artifacts.
- **Teams and projects** needing collaborative storage/management of brain data and simulation campaigns (public vs. project modes, workspace configuration).
- **Educators and students** using standardized example notebooks and workflows to learn model building and simulation.

### Real‑world applications (concrete examples drawn from docs)
- Research workflows:
  - Build and register ion channel models from electrophysiological recordings (generate .mod mechanisms; register IonChannelModel entities).
  - Skeletonize and analyze neuron morphologies and prepare morphologies for simulation.
  - Configure and run paired-neuron simulations to study synaptic transmission and short‑term plasticity.
  - Design and execute circuit simulations (microcircuits) to study network dynamics, connectivity patterns, and emergent behaviors.
- Reproducible science and provenance:
  - Share, reproduce and audit modeling workflows and simulation campaigns with standardized provenance and entity registration.
- Comparative and integrative studies:
  - Combine experimental datasets (morphology, electrophysiology, synapse maps) with modeling to test hypotheses about mechanisms (e.g., how channel changes alter circuit activity).
- Tooling & automation:
  - Parameter scans and batch simulation campaigns to explore model parameter spaces systematically.
  - Programmatic access (OBI-one API) enabling integration into analysis pipelines, notebooks, and CI-type model testing workflows.
- Education and training:
  - Example notebooks and standardized workflows for hands‑on training in neuron/circuit modeling.

### How the platform supports these uses (features emphasized in docs)
- Centralized **Explore Data** interface for discovering data, models and literature.
- **OBI-one** workflows and FastAPI service for standardized, reproducible model-building pipelines (ion channels, skeletonization, paired/circuit simulations).
- Support for both **web UI** and **programmatic** usage to accommodate interactive and automated workflows.
- Facilities for parameter scans, recording configurations, neuron set selection and synaptic manipulations when configuring simulations.

Short answer: yes — the platform provides a model-building and simulation workflow (Build + Simulate) that you can use as the basis for assembling and running circuit-scale models. Below I outline the platform features and a recommended high-level workflow for assembling a fly brain model using the platform (workflows, model picker, configuration, campaigns, and credits). If you want, I can now search the documentation and project catalog specifically for *Drosophila* / fly connectome datasets, example fly circuits, or community models to import — would you like me to do that?

---

## Key platform capabilities (from product docs)

- **Workflows tab**  
  - Central place to start work: **Build** (construct models) and **Simulate** (run simulations).  
  - A two-level picker: first choose Category (Build / Simulate), then choose a Type (scale/flavor).  
  - Activity listing below shows existing workflows for your project.

- **Types currently exposed (examples)**  
  - Under **Build**: Single neuron, Synaptome, Ion channel (others shown as roadmap items).  
  - Under **Simulate**: Single neuron, Synaptome, Paired neurons (beta), Small microcircuit (beta), and beta variants for next-gen flows.

- **Model picker & preview**  
  - After selecting a Type, pick a model from Public or Project models.  
  - Preview shows morphology/trace thumbnails, metadata (brain region, m-type/e-type for single-neuron flows), and allows **Use model** to enter configuration.  
  - For circuit-scale picks (pairs, microcircuits) the picker shows circuit-level metadata: number of neurons, synapses, connections.

- **Configuration workspace (Simulation design)**  
  - Three-column UI: left = section navigator (Info, Init, Stimuli, Recordings, Manipulations, Events); middle = forms; right = live model preview.  
  - Required metadata: **Campaign name** and **Campaign description**.  
  - **Initialization** sets duration, time step, initial voltage, extracellular conditions, random seed.  
  - **Stimuli**: many stimulus types supported (relative to threshold or absolute).  
  - **Recordings**: choose what to record (soma voltage, windows, sampling timesteps).  
  - **Manipulations**: neuronal or synaptic manipulations (optional).  
  - **Events / Timestamps**: define named time markers for synchronizing stimuli.  
  - **Parameter scans**: numeric fields can be converted to scan values (plus-circle icon) to generate multiple simulations from one campaign (Cartesian product of scan values).  
  - Generate simulations only after every required section shows valid (green) status.

- **Campaign generation & simulations tab**  
  - Clicking **Generate simulation(s)** validates the campaign and creates one Simulation per scanned-parameter combination.  
  - The **Simulations** tab lists generated simulations; you can launch, monitor, and download outputs once they run.

- **Virtual Lab & credits**  
  - Work is organized inside Virtual Labs and Projects. Credits are required to run workflows; Owners/Admins can allocate credits to projects. The UI shows credit balance and estimated costs.

---

## Recommended high-level workflow to build a fly brain on this platform

(These steps use the platform features above. I can expand or run targeted doc searches for each step if you want.)

1. Project & data setup
   - Create (or use) a Virtual Lab and Project to hold your fly model work.
   - Ensure credits are available (owner/admin may need to allocate).

2. Inventory available fly connectome datasets / models
   - Search the platform Public models (model picker) and project assets for existing *Drosophila* reconstructions, connectomes, or circuit models (use the Model Picker under Build/Simulate).
   - If you have external connectome files (EM reconstructions, graph/Sonata outputs), plan how to map them onto the platform’s model formats (the platform exposes Build workflows and import options — I can search docs for import formats).

3. Build / assemble components
   - Use **Build → Synaptome / Single neuron / Ion channel** (as appropriate) to create required building blocks:
     - Neuron morphologies and electrical models (single-neuron ME-models).
     - Synaptome / synapse definitions (to represent connection physiology).
   - For circuit-scale assembly, either:
     - Import an existing circuit model (if available in Public or your Project), or
     - Build circuits by composing neuron models + synaptome definitions (Build workflows are the starting point).

4. Map connectome graph to platform circuit
   - Convert your connectome adjacency / synapse data to the platform’s circuit representation (the platform supports circuit-scale workflows and circuit models). If you need, I can search documentation for supported import formats (Sonata, JSON, etc.) or example workflows that show how to ingest connectome-derived connectivity.

5. Validate & sample

6. Configure simulation campaigns
   - Open **Simulate**, choose the appropriate Type (paired, microcircuit, or single-neuron workflows depending on scale), pick your circuit/model from the Model Picker, then configure:
     - Info (campaign metadata), Initialization (duration, dt, extracellular ionic conditions), Stimuli, Recordings, Manipulations, Events.

7. Generate simulations & run
   - Generate the campaign (creates simulations per scan combinations), then launch runs from the Simulations tab.
   - Monitor runs and download results for analysis.

8. Iterate & scale
   - Start with smaller subcircuits or down-sampled versions of the fly connectome to validate pipeline and costs, then scale up to larger assemblies as needed.
   - Use parameter scans and manipulations to test hypotheses about connectivity, synaptic strength, or neuromodulation.

calcium_plateau_potentials_simulation.user: I want to simulate a neuron and see plateau potentials in the apical dendrite due to calcium. What ME model and simulation parameters should I use?
calcium_plateau_potentials_simulation.expected_output: Select a hippocampal pyramidal ME-model. At present, the public rat and mouse hippocampus ME-Models have the brain region `Field CA1`, e-type `cACpyr` (for rat models and `cADpyr` for mouse models) and m-type `SP_PC`. Select one such pyramidal neuron model. I should include calcium ion channel mechanisms, then configure a simulation with current injection into distal apical dendrite section and set recording locations along the apical dendrite to capture voltage traces. calcium_plateau_potentials_simulation.ai_response:
I'll check the platform documentation for single-cell simulation guidance and recommendations for ME-models and parameters. I'll search the docs now.I'll search the ME-Model catalog for models mentioning calcium or apical dendrite.I'll retry the ME-Model search without brain-region directional parameters.## Summary / next step
I can (A) recommend a concrete set of ME‑model features and simulation parameters you should use to evoke and record calcium‑mediated plateau potentials in the apical dendrite, and (B) optionally search the platform ME‑Model catalog for candidate ME‑models (e.g., L5 pyramidal cells with apical tuft and calcium mechanisms). Which would you like? If you want me to search the catalog, tell me the species and brain region (or say “any”) — the catalog search requires a brain-region scope to return results.

Below I give a focused, ready-to-use recipe you can apply in the platform’s single‑neuron (ME‑model) simulation flow.

---

## Mechanistic idea (brief)
Plateau potentials in apical dendrites are typically produced by a local regenerative inward calcium current (often L‑type / high‑voltage activated Ca2+ channels and/or persistent Na+) together with intracellular Ca2+ accumulation and interactions with Ca2+‑activated K+ currents (e.g., SK). To elicit and observe a plateau you:
- provide a strong, localized depolarizing drive to the distal apical tuft/branch,
- ensure the model contains realistic Ca2+ channels and intracellular Ca2+ handling,
- optionally reduce Ca2+‑activated K+ or increase Ca2+ channel density in the apical compartments,
- record apical dendritic voltage and Ca2+ concentration with good spatial and temporal sampling.

---

## Recommended ME‑model features to choose
- **Cell type**: a cortical pyramidal neuron with a pronounced apical dendrite / tuft (e.g., **L5 pyramidal** or other layer 5/2/3 pyramidal cell).
- **Morphology**: full reconstructed morphology including apical trunk and tuft branches (able to target distal apical compartments).
- **Electrophysiology / mechanisms**:
  - Includes voltage‑gated calcium channels (HV A‑type / L‑type like Cav1, and possibly Cav2 family) expressed in apical dendrites.
  - Includes intracellular Ca2+ dynamics (a Ca2+ pool with realistic decay τ and buffering).
  - Contains Ca2+‑dependent K+ channels (e.g., SK) so you can modulate plateau termination.
  - Prefer validated ME‑models (Validated = True in model metadata) if available.
- **Assets visible in model preview**: morphology thumbnail and example traces (helpful).

If you want me to search the catalog for matching ME‑models, tell me species and brain-region to scope the search (or say “any”).

---

## Simulation configuration (single‑neuron ME simulation)
Use the Single neuron (beta) simulation flow. Key fields:

- **ME Model**: pick the L5 (or similar) pyramidal ME‑model with apical tuft and Ca mechanisms.
- **Duration**: 1500–3000 ms (use 2000 ms as a typical starting point).
- **Time step (dt / Recording timestep)**:
  - Simulation integrator step: 0.025–0.1 ms. Start with **dt = 0.025 ms** (captures fast spikes and dendritic events).
  - Recording timestep: 0.05–0.1 ms (0.05 ms recommended for detailed AP/dendritic dynamics).
- **Temperature**: use the model default (or physiological ~ 34–37 °C) unless the model specifies otherwise.
- **Extracellular Calcium concentration** (global param shown in Initialization): platform default is **1.1 mM**; you can try 1.1 (physiological) and 2.0 mM (higher release/probability) if synaptic release matters — but plateau potentials are primarily intrinsic here, so extracellular Ca mainly matters for Ca2+ dependent mechanisms if included.

---

## Stimuli — how to drive the apical dendrite to produce a plateau
- Add a **dendritic current clamp** targeted to the distal apical tuft or apical branch sections:
  - Stimulus type: If available, choose a Somatic vs Dendritic clamp that allows specifying *Neuron set or SectionList* targeting apical tuft. In Single neuron (beta) you may need to use the neuronal manipulation or a clamp targeted to the apical compartments (Paired/Small microcircuit flows expose neuron set selection; single‑neuron beta may assume single cell — check Stimulus form for a Neuron set / SectionList field).
  - If the stimulus can be applied to a specific compartment: choose sections mapping to apical tuft (SectionList: apical_dendrite, apical_tuft, or similar).
- Stimulus waveform (two often‑used protocols):
  1. **Strong distal depolarizing pulse**:
     - Type: Constant current clamp (Relative or Absolute).
     - Duration: 200–600 ms (pulse long enough to permit Ca2+ accumulation).
     - Amplitude: If Relative clamp is available, try **60–150% of threshold** for the distal compartment (note: thresholds are usually soma‑based; if Relative is only soma‑scaled use a high percentage and/or apply to dendrite). If Absolute: start with 0.05–1.0 nA depending on compartment size — tune by small scans.
     - Timestamp: start ~100–200 ms into the simulation (allow baseline).
  2. **Ramp / step** to recruit Ca channels:
     - Type: Linear Somatic/Dendritic Current Clamp (Relative or Absolute).
     - Ramp Duration: 50–200 ms up to a depolarized plateau and then hold for 200–500 ms.
- Use scans to sweep current amplitude (e.g., two or three amplitudes) to identify the threshold for plateau initiation.

---

## Manipulations (to promote/verify calcium dependence)
- **Increase apical L‑type Ca channel conductance** (if model exposes channel variables):
  - Sectionwise modification: modify the Ca channel gbar variable for apical sections (e.g., multiply gbar by 1.5–3×) to make dendrite more regenerative.
- **Reduce Ca2+‑activated K+ (SK) conductance**:
  - Decrease SK gbar in apical sections (e.g., 0.5×) to make plateau less easily terminated — useful as an experimental manipulation to test Ca dependence.
- If the model provides a Ca2+ buffer/decay time constant: increasing intracellular Ca decay time (slower Ca clearance) tends to support longer plateaus.

In the beta flow these are done via the Manipulations → Neuronal manipulations section (either “Variable Modification by Section List” or “Full Neuron Variable Modification”); choose section lists corresponding to apical compartments.

---

## Recordings (what to save)
- **Apical dendrite voltage**: record at least one distal apical compartment (apical trunk near tuft, and a tuft branch). Use multiple recordings if possible:
  - Name: Apical distal V_m
  - Timestep: 0.05 ms
- **Somatic voltage**: record soma V_m simultaneously to observe backpropagation and global spike coupling.
- **Intracellular [Ca2+]** (if supported): record Ca2+ concentration in the same apical compartments (and soma optionally).
- **Channel currents** (optional): record L‑type Ca current (I_Ca_L) and Ca‑activated K currents (I_SK) in apical sections to confirm mechanisms.
- **Events**: mark stimulus onset/offset with Timestamps so you can align traces easily.

---

## Parameter scan suggestions
Keep scans modest (each extra value multiplies simulations):

- Vary stimulus amplitude (e.g., 3 values: subthreshold, near threshold, suprathreshold).
- Vary apical Ca channel gbar (baseline, 2×).
- Vary SK conductance (baseline, 0.5×).
- Optionally sweep intracellular Ca decay τ (fast vs slow).

Start with a 2×2 or 3×2 grid to quickly identify regimes that produce plateaus.

---

## Example minimal experiment (values to paste in the beta form)
- Initialization:
  - Duration: **2000 ms**
  - Extracellular Calcium: **1.1 mM**
  - Initial Voltage: **-80 mV**
  - Random Seed: 1
- Stimulus 0:
  - Type: Constant Dendritic Current Clamp (Relative if available)
  - Duration: **400 ms**
  - Timestamp: **Event at 100 ms** (so stimulus fires 100–500 ms)
  - Percentage of Threshold (if Relative): **100%** and scan to **140%**
  - Target: distal apical tuft SectionList
- Recordings:
  - Soma V_m (dt 0.05 ms)
  - Apical distal V_m (dt 0.05 ms)
  - Apical [Ca2+] (dt 1.0–5.0 ms if Ca trace is slower; or 0.5 ms if needed)
- Manipulation (optional):
  - Neuronal manipulation: increase apical L‑type Ca gbar × **2.0**
  - Or reduce apical SK gbar × **0.5**
- Output: Inspect apical voltage traces for a long depolarized plateau that outlasts the stimulus or persists until Ca‑activated K current builds up.

---

## How to confirm the plateau is Ca‑dependent
- Run the baseline simulation (no manipulation) to see if plateau appears.
- Then:
  1. **Block Ca channels**: set apical Ca channel gbar to 0 or very low — plateau should disappear.
  2. **Increase SK**: increase apical SK conductance — plateau should be shortened or abolished.
  3. Compare apical [Ca2+] traces: plateau should be correlated with sustained Ca elevation.
  4. Inspect I_Ca_L and I_SK currents during plateau: sustained inward Ca and delayed outward SK signature confirms mechanism.

---

## Practical tips & troubleshooting
- If you see only somatic spikes and no dendritic plateau: move the stimulus further distal, increase amplitude or duration, or increase dendritic Ca channel density.
- If simulation is unstable at very small dt, try dt = 0.025–0.05 ms and check numerical options in the model (some ME models require small dt).
- Use the beta flow’s section lists to precisely target apical compartments — naming neuron sets or SectionLists makes targeting easier.
- Start with fewer recorded variables to save output size; add more once you see interesting events.

---

## Would you like me to...
- A — Search the platform ME‑Model catalog for candidate models (e.g., “L5 pyramidal” or “apical tuft”)? If so, please specify species and brain region (or say “any”) and I’ll search and return matching models and links.
- B — Generate a ready‑to‑paste parameter checklist for the Single neuron (beta) configuration UI (I can format fields exactly as the UI expects)?
- C — Walk you step‑by‑step through setting this up in the Workflows → Simulate → Single neuron (beta) UI?

Tell me which option you prefer (A, B, or C) and — for A — the species/brain region to scope the search.

m_vs_e_model_explanation.user: Explain the difference between m-model and e-model.
m_vs_e_model_explanation.expected_output:
The m-model is a detailed 3D reconstructed or synthesised morphology. A 3D reconstructed morphology is generated by tracing a microscopic images of biological neurons. The images are usally obtained using usally thin slices of tissue plated on a glass. Some reconstructed morphologies are obtained by `skeletonisation` of electron microscopic meshses (e.g. MiCRONS morphology meshes). The reconstructed morphologies usually undergo a pipeline to correct and fix for problems which can cause issues during simualtions. Synthesised morphologies are generated by topological synthesis using features from multiple morphologies. 
An e-model or electrical model is a usually a NEURON simulator-based single neuron model which is constructed by adding ion channel models to an reconstructed or synthesised morphology to generate electrical activity. The e-models are optimised using multi-objective optimisation (e.g. using BluePyEModel or BluePyOpt) to generate specific firing patterns as seen in real electrophysiological recordings. These models are also validated by using protocols and electrical features or e-features (e.g. using eFEL python library) which were not used for optimisation. E-models form a template for build more such neuron models by combining with morphologies (m-model) to generate me-models.  The main differences between an e-model and me-model is that e-models are optimised and non-simulatable (on the platform). Whereas me-models are formed by combining m-model and e-model, and can be simulated on the platform. The me-models are not optimised for any cobination of m-model and e-model.
m_vs_e_model_explanation.ai_response:
## Summary — short answer
- **M-model (morphology model):** describes a neuron's physical shape — soma, dendrites, axon — usually as a reconstructed tree of compartments.  
- **E-model (electrical model / ion-channel model):** describes a neuron's membrane electrophysiology — ion channels, conductances, kinetics — without necessarily including a full morphology.  

They are complementary: an **ME-model** (morphology + electrical) combines both to produce a morphologically-detailed, biophysically-realistic neuron ready for simulation.

---

## Detailed comparison

### 1) What each contains
- **M-model (Morphology model)**
  - Geometric structure: 3D coordinates, radii, branching topology for soma, dendrites, axon.
  - Compartmentalization information (how the morphology is split into sections/segments).
  - Often derived from reconstructions (e.g., Neurolucida, SWC files).
  - Typically *no* ion-channel kinetics or membrane properties embedded (or only simple passive properties).

- **E-model (Electrical model / Ion-channel model)**
  - Sets of ion channel descriptions (gates, kinetics, reversal potentials).
  - Conductances, densities, kinetics equations (Hodgkin–Huxley style or Markov models).
  - Passive electrical properties (Rm, Cm, Ra) and often compartment-independent parameters.
  - May be specified for somatic or point-neuron models (single-compartment) or used as channel templates for use with morphologies.

### 2) Level of biophysical detail & intended use
- **M-model**
  - Focus: spatial structure, anatomical analyses (branch length, dendritic geometry, synapse placement).
  - Use cases: morphological statistics, connectivity mapping, placing synapses on realistic arbors, generating ME-models.

- **E-model**
  - Focus: membrane dynamics, excitability, firing patterns, channel contributions.
  - Use cases: reproducing electrophysiological recordings, channel-level studies, building point-neuron or multi-compartment electrical models.

### 3) Typical file formats / representations
- **M-model:** SWC, Neurolucida, MorphML — files that encode tree structure and 3D coordinates.
- **E-model:** Channel model files (NMODL, mod files), JSON/SONATA descriptions of channel parameters, or textual kinetic equations.

### 4) How they combine: the ME-model
- **ME-model = M-model + E-model**
  - Morphology provides spatial compartments; electrical properties (ion channels, passive parameters) are assigned across those compartments (possibly with spatial gradients).
  - This is the canonical input for detailed compartmental simulators (NEURON, GENESIS, Arbor) to simulate realistic voltage responses and synaptic integration.

### 5) Strengths & limitations
- **M-model**
  - Strength: anatomical realism, necessary for spatially-resolved synaptic input and cable filtering.
  - Limitation: alone it cannot predict firing or subthreshold dynamics beyond simple passive behavior.

- **E-model**
  - Strength: captures dynamics of excitability and ionic mechanisms even in abstract/simple neuron representations.
  - Limitation: without morphology, it cannot model location-dependent phenomena (dendritic spikes, input attenuation, local synapse effects).

### 6) Practical examples
- A lab reconstructs dendrites from an intracellular fill → exports an **m-model** (SWC).  
- A electrophysiologist fits channel kinetics to patch recordings → produces an **e-model** (set of ion-channel mechanisms and conductance densities).  
- A modeler merges both to create an **ME-model** and runs a compartmental simulation to reproduce in vivo-like responses.

---

## Quick checklist to decide which you need
- Want realistic synapse placement or dendritic computations? → need an **M-model** (and likely ME-model).  
- Want to study channel contributions to spike shape or adaptation in a point neuron? → an **E-model** may suffice.  
- Want both spatial and biophysical realism for simulations? → build an **ME-model**.

---

If you'd like, I can:
- Show typical file examples (SWC for morphology, NMODL for channels), or

## Permissions & access
